exercises.title: A: Gap Fill (Book Review)
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Seldom (1) ...... a reader encounter such an incisive examination of urban inequality as Marcus Webb's Divided Cities.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is inversion after Seldom, so the correct answer is "does".

wrongOptions.1: The bare infinitive (1) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (1) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: Those who choose to bury their heads in the (2) ...... and pretend that housing inequality is no longer an issue will find Webb's data deeply unsettling.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is idiom, so the correct answer is "sand".

wrongOptions.2: The bare infinitive (2) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Choose the future form that matches whether the sentence predicts, plans, or arranges.

items.sentence: Webb persuasively demonstrates that (3) ...... only have those (4) ...... power consolidated their influence, but lower-income communities are also less able to stand (5) ...... to developers.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not only… but also, so the correct answer is "not, in, up".

wrongOptions.3: The bare infinitive (3) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (3) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: At (6) ...... stage in the book (7) ...... Webb's assertions made without supporting data, and at no point does he talk (8) ...... to readers unfamiliar with the topic.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is at no stage, so the correct answer is "no, are, down".

wrongOptions.6: The bare infinitive (6) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (6) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: His overarching argument, that (9) ...... when citizens unite and challenge the powers that (10) ...... will meaningful reform become possible, is both compelling and timely.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is only when, so the correct answer is "only, be".

wrongOptions.9: The bare infinitive (9) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Choose the future form that matches whether the sentence predicts, plans, or arranges.

exercises.title: B: Word Formation
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals.

items.sentence: Repeated exposure to cold temperatures can ...... (HARD) the skin, making it more resistant to minor abrasions.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is verb from HARD, so the correct answer is "harden".

wrongOptions.hard: The root HARD cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "harden".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of HARD that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: HARD → harden: Use the -ing form for an activity or gerund.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "harden" is the word-formation answer from the root HARD in this context.

items.sentence: The coach's dedication to fair play ...... (EXAMPLE) the values that the entire academy strives to uphold.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is verb from EXAMPLE, so the correct answer is "exemplifies".

wrongOptions.example: The root EXAMPLE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "exemplifies".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of EXAMPLE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: EXAMPLE → exemplifies: Use the -ing form for an activity or gerund.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "exemplifies" is the word-formation answer from the root EXAMPLE in this context.

items.sentence: Several volunteers felt ...... (MORAL) after the funding for the community project was unexpectedly withdrawn.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is negative MORAL, so the correct answer is "demoralised".

wrongOptions.moral: The root MORAL cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "demoralised".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of MORAL that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: MORAL → demoralised: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "demoralised" is the word-formation answer from the root MORAL in this context.

items.sentence: Victims of online fraud often describe a lasting feeling of ...... (POWER) when they realise they cannot recover their money.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from POWER, so the correct answer is "powerlessness".

wrongOptions.power: The root POWER cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "powerlessness".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of POWER that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: POWER → powerlessness: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "powerlessness" is the word-formation answer from the root POWER in this context.

items.sentence: There is an ongoing debate about whether it would be beneficial to ...... (CRIME) certain minor traffic offences.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is remove criminal status, so the correct answer is "decriminalise".

wrongOptions.crime: The root CRIME cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "decriminalise".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of CRIME that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: CRIME → decriminalise: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "decriminalise" is the word-formation answer from the root CRIME in this context.

items.sentence: A single vote might appear ...... (SIGNIFY) in a national election, yet the collective impact of voter turnout is enormous.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is negative SIGNIFY, so the correct answer is "insignificant".

wrongOptions.signify: The root SIGNIFY cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "insignificant".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of SIGNIFY that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: SIGNIFY → insignificant: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "insignificant" is the word-formation answer from the root SIGNIFY in this context.

items.sentence: The security guard was dismissed after striking a customer without any apparent ...... (PROVOKE) .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from PROVOKE, so the correct answer is "provocation".

wrongOptions.provoke: The root PROVOKE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "provocation".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of PROVOKE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: PROVOKE → provocation: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "provocation" is the word-formation answer from the root PROVOKE in this context.

items.sentence: After years of internal conflict, the region had become virtually ...... (GOVERN) and required international intervention.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is negative GOVERN, so the correct answer is "ungovernable".

wrongOptions.govern: The root GOVERN cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "ungovernable".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of GOVERN that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: GOVERN → ungovernable: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "ungovernable" is the word-formation answer from the root GOVERN in this context.

exercises.title: C: Collocations / Prepositions
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: She avoids checking her phone during dinner ...... a rule, preferring to focus on conversation.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is fixed phrase, so the correct answer is "as".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "as" collocates naturally with the words around the gap in this context.

items.sentence: Am I right ...... thinking that the conference has been moved to a different venue this year?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is am I right, so the correct answer is "in".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence: The merger will proceed, but it remains subject ...... approval by the financial regulatory body.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is subject to, so the correct answer is "to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: He ...... his time for the offence and was determined to rebuild his life upon release.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is serve a sentence, so the correct answer is "served".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence: All confidential documents are kept ...... lock and key in the archive room on the ground floor.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is secure storage, so the correct answer is "under".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: The court reminded the defendant that no one, regardless of status, is ...... the law.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is nobody is above, so the correct answer is "above".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: After months of negotiations, the militant faction agreed to lay ...... their weapons and enter peace talks.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is surrender weapons, so the correct answer is "down".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: The director decided to ...... an example of the employee who had leaked confidential information.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is punish as warning, so the correct answer is "make".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

exercises.title: D: Key Word Transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence:
There are no circumstances in which volunteers may enter the restricted zone without prior clearance. **under**
Volunteers ...... the restricted zone without prior clearance.
items.answer: are under no circumstances to enter
acceptedAnswers: are under no circumstances to enter

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is no circumstances, so the correct answer is "are under no circumstances to enter".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "are under no circumstances to enter", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
I would advise against confronting the director over this issue. **horns**
I would advise against ...... the director over this issue.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is argue fiercely, so the correct answer is "locking horns with".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "locking horns with", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
The delegates could only vote once the chairperson had formally opened the session. **after**
Only ...... vote.
items.answer: after the chairperson had formally opened the session could the delegates
acceptedAnswers: after the chairperson had formally opened the session could the delegates

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is inversion after Only, so the correct answer is "after the chairperson had formally opened the session could the delegates".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "after the chairperson had formally opened the session could the delegates", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
The system would not let the students view their individual results. **denied**
The system ...... their individual results.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is deny access, so the correct answer is "denied the students access to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "denied the students access to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
The fire alarm went off as soon as the technician entered the building. **had**
No ...... the fire alarm went off.
items.answer: sooner had the technician entered the building than
acceptedAnswers: sooner had the technician entered the building than

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is no sooner… than, so the correct answer is "sooner had the technician entered the building than".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "sooner had the technician entered the building than", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
A trustworthy insider informed me that the factory is about to close permanently. **authority**
I ...... that the factory is about to close permanently.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is reliable source, so the correct answer is "have it on good authority".

wrongOptions.it on good authority: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "have it on good authority", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
There was such widespread anger at the verdict that protests broke out across the city. **so**
Protests broke out across the city, ...... the verdict.
items.answer: so widespread was the anger at
acceptedAnswers: so widespread was the anger at

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is so + inversion, so the correct answer is "so widespread was the anger at".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "so widespread was the anger at", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
Why do you think you can speak to your colleagues in that manner? **gives**
What do you think ...... to speak to your colleagues in that manner?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is right to do something, so the correct answer is "gives you the right".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "gives you the right", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentenceBefore: The new planning regulations have generated considerable
items.sentenceAfter: among residents and local business owners alike.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "controversy" — public disagreement.

wrongOptions.B: "consent" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "controversy" is correct.
wrongOptions.consent: "consent" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "controversy" is correct.
wrongOptions.C: "conformity" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "controversy" is correct.
wrongOptions.conformity: "conformity" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "controversy" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "consequence" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "controversy" is correct.
wrongOptions.consequence: "consequence" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "controversy" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Eliminate options that break grammar first, then check which remaining word fits the meaning.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "controversy" is the only option that matches both meaning and register in this sentence.

items.sentenceBefore: This neighbourhood struggles with anti-social behaviour,
items.sentenceAfter: do several other districts in the region.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "as" — as do.

wrongOptions.A: "so" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "as" is correct.
wrongOptions.so: "so" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "as" is correct.
wrongOptions.B: "nor" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "as" is correct.
wrongOptions.nor: "nor" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "as" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "like" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "as" is correct.
wrongOptions.like: "like" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "as" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Time expressions like for, since, already, and yet are strong present-perfect signals.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links past actions to now — look for markers like for, since, already, yet, or ever.

wrongOptions.A: "hit" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "crack" is correct.

wrongOptions.B: "force" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "crack" is correct.

items.sentenceBefore: If the product arrives damaged, you have
items.sentenceAfter: right to request a full refund from the retailer.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is fixed phrase, so the correct answer is "every".

wrongOptions.A: "all" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "every" fits better.
wrongOptions.all: "all" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "every" fits better.
wrongOptions.B: "each" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "every" fits better.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Fixed phrases must be learned as whole chunks — individual words may not translate literally.
